consensus: React learning must start with JavaScript fundamentals (ES6+, closures, async/await, array methods) before touching React itself.
Core React progression: JSX → components → props → useState → useEffect → composition → custom hooks.
Every phase must be anchored to a concrete hands-on project — not passive tutorial consumption.
Hook rules (no conditional calls, no loops) and stale closures in useEffect are the two most critical early pitfalls to flag.
TypeScript should be introduced after core React concepts are comfortable, not simultaneously — typically after 3-4 phases.
CRA (Create React App) is deprecated; Vite is the recommended bootstrap tool.
Official React docs (react.dev) are the canonical primary resource; all paths should anchor there.
State management libraries (Redux, Zustand, etc.) should be deferred until the app demonstrates a genuine need beyond useState/Context/useReducer.
The path should end with testing (Vitest + React Testing Library), deployment, and a production capstone (Next.js or similar).
A portfolio of 4-5 original projects is more valuable than any certificate or course completion badge.

contradictions.details: Kimi-K2.7 and Qwen3.6-27B say introduce TypeScript after Phase 3-4 (M4 or Phase 7). Qwen3.6-35B-A3B says after Phase 3 (i.e., earlier). MiniMax-M3 critiques scope but doesn't specify a phase. The core disagreement is whether 'after M4' (later, once React patterns are solid) or 'after Phase 3' (sooner, before building complex apps) is optimal.
contradictions.recommended_resolution: Adopt 'soft introduction after Phase 3, full adoption by Phase 7' — use .tsx for new small projects once hooks are comfortable, but don't retrofit existing work. This reconciles both positions: it's not a hard gate, but TypeScript is in play before complex apps are built.

contradictions.details: Kimi-K2.7 suggests delaying library choice until the app demonstrates a state-management problem. Qwen3.6-27B suggests using Context + useReducer in Phase 3 and defines Phase 5 as 'State management (Zustand or Redux Toolkit)'. Qwen3.6-35B-A3B lists Zustand/Redux Toolkit as Phase 5 but adds 'most apps don't need a global store'. The contradiction is whether to set a hard Phase 5 for introducing a library or keep it conditional.
contradictions.recommended_resolution: Keep state management library introduction conditional (Kimi-K2.7's approach). Phase 5 should be labeled 'Optional: State Management Libraries' with a litmus test: 'if you have 3+ deeply nested components sharing state and Context causes re-render cascades, then evaluate Zustand.' This avoids premature adoption.

contradictions.details: Qwen3.6-27B estimates 8-14 weeks, Qwen3.6-35B-A3B estimates 8-12 weeks. The difference is 2 weeks on the upper bound. This is minor but indicates different assumptions about depth vs velocity.
contradictions.recommended_resolution: Use 8-14 weeks as the more conservative (and therefore safer) estimate. The 8-week lower bound assumes full-time-ish dedication; 14 weeks accommodates part-time learners.

contradictions.details: Kimi-K2.7 flags Next.js App Router as volatile/stabilizing and recommends understanding the old client-first model before jumping in. Qwen3.6-27B puts Next.js in Phase 8 as the capstone without that caveat. MiniMax-M3 notes 'a path older than ~12 months may be stale' as a risk. The contradiction is whether to warn learners about App Router churn or simply use it.
contradictions.recommended_resolution: Include the Kimi-K2.7 caveat explicitly: 'The Next.js App Router mental model (server/client boundary, caching semantics) is still stabilizing. Before Phase 8, ensure you can debug which code runs on the server vs client — this is the #1 source of confusion.'

missing_coverage: No model addresses React Native or mobile development as a sub-path — all paths are web-only. 'React' can mean React Native for mobile teams, and this should be flagged as a fork point.
No model provides concrete time-boxing per phase (e.g., 'Phase 2: 15-20 hours'). Qwen3.6-27B gives total timeline but not per-phase breakdown.

Accessibility (a11y) is mentioned by Qwen3.6-35B-A3B as a low-risk note and by MiniMax-M3 in the rubric, but no model provides concrete a11y checkpoints or exercises (screen reader testing, keyboard navigation, ARIA audit).
Security (XSS/CSRF) is only mentioned by MiniMax-M3 in the rubric; no other model flags it. For a production path, this is a gap.

No model addresses AI-assisted learning as a double-edged sword — MiniMax-M3 mentions 'over-reliance on AI codegen' in its caveats section but no other model explores it as a pedagogical risk or tool.
GraphQL is mentioned only once (Qwen3.6-27B says 'avoid GraphQL until REST is solid') but REST-to-GraphQL migration path is not in any model's progression.
No model provides a 'what to do when stuck' strategy — debugging workflow, how to read error messages, React DevTools usage, or community resources (Discord, Stack Overflow).
CSS-in-JS vs Tailwind vs CSS Modules — Qwen3.6-27B says 'pick one', but no model compares the tradeoffs or gives a decision framework.
Backend integration depth — all models assume a frontend-first path. No model discusses how a full-stack learner should sequence backend (Node/Express, DB) alongside React.

unique_insights.insight: Frames React learning as an architectural progression map with a 'retain the core model' thesis: unidirectional, reactive component tree that reconciles declarative UI descriptions. This mental-model-first framing is unique and correct — it prevents learners from treating React as a black-box framework.

Provides concrete self-assessment heuristics per phase ('Am I ready for the next phase?') including: explain every useEffect dependency, refactor a class-ish component into idiomatic hooks, trace a state change through the component tree, identify which parts run on server vs client in Next.js.

Defines 'audience gate' as the first evaluation criterion — the path must ask/assume JS/HTML/CSS proficiency, TypeScript comfort, and prior framework exposure before proceeding. This prevents one-size-fits-all answers that fail learners with different backgrounds.

Explicitly calls for a 'code review test' as a validation gate: submit one project to a human reviewer or community and pass without major rewrites. This is the strongest quality-assurance mechanism proposed and no other model includes it.

Provides the most granular and phase-specific project ladder with concrete technologies per phase (React Query in Phase 4, Tailwind/CSS Modules in Phase 5, Error Boundaries and Portals in Phase 6, Prisma + PostgreSQL in Phase 8). This is the most actionable 'build this' mapping.

The only model to provide concrete self-assessment questions that test understanding rather than recall: 'explain prop drilling vs context without looking', 'build a search with debounced API calls using only useState/useEffect', 'explain useMemo vs useCallback with a case where each is actually necessary'. These are directly usable as phase-exit exams.

Identifies 'useEffect overuse for data fetching' as a learning stepping-stone, not a production default — and explicitly recommends React Query/SWR or Server Components as the modern replacement. This is a critical modernization signal that other models touch but don't emphasize as strongly.

risks.risk: The panel produced a React learning path for an unknown original request. If the original user had a specific constraint (e.g., 'I have 2 weeks and know Vue already', 'I'm building a React Native app'), this path is overfit for a web-only, 8-14 week beginner.
risks.mitigation: The final response must open with 2-3 clarifying questions OR explicitly state assumptions (background: JS-competent beginner, target: web SPA → full-stack, timeline: 8-14 weeks).

risks.risk: Next.js App Router churn risk is under-weighted by Qwen3.6-27B and Qwen3.6-35B-A3B. If the path recommends a specific Next.js version and the API changes within 6 months, the capstone becomes a debugging nightmare.
risks.mitigation: Pin to Next.js 14+ (stable App Router) and link to the official migration guide. Warn that 15+ may introduce further breaking changes and recommend checking release notes before the capstone.

risks.risk: All models assume functional-components-only. While correct, this creates a gap when learners encounter class components in legacy codebases or older tutorials. Zero exposure to class component syntax means they can't read/refactor existing code.
risks.mitigation: Add a brief 'Legacy Awareness' sidebar: show one class component with lifecycle methods, explain how it maps to hooks, and state that this is for reading legacy code only — never for new projects.

risks.risk: The consensus on 'defer state management libraries' may lead learners to over-use Context API and cause re-render performance issues. Context is not a state management tool — it's a dependency injection mechanism.
risks.mitigation: Explicitly distinguish: 'Context is for dependency injection (theme, locale, auth user). For shared mutable state that changes frequently, use useReducer + lift state, or evaluate an external store like Zustand.'

risks.risk: No model provides a concrete debugging workflow or React DevTools tutorial. Learners will hit errors and not know how to self-diagnose. This is the #1 dropout point.
risks.mitigation: Add a Phase 2.5 or interleaved module: 'Debugging React' covering React DevTools (Components tab, Profiler), reading stack traces, strict mode double-mounting, and common error messages (e.g., 'Rendered fewer hooks than expected').

recommended_final_strategy: The panel strongly converges on a project-driven, phase-gated path from JS fundamentals through React hooks to Next.js deployment. There are no fatal contradictions — only timing disagreements (TypeScript, state libraries, Next.js) that can be resolved with conditional gates. The final response should: (1) Open with explicit learner-background assumptions or 2-3 clarifying questions. (2) Present the architectural progression map from Kimi-K2.7 as the skeleton. (3) Layer Qwen3.6-27B's concrete project-per-phase mapping onto it. (4) Use Qwen3.6-35B-A3B's self-assessment questions as phase-exit exams. (5) Inject MiniMax-M3's code-review-gate and audience-gate criteria as quality checks. (6) Flag CRA as deprecated, Vite as the default, and React.dev as canonical. (7) Include a 'Legacy Awareness' sidebar for class components and a 'Debugging React' interleaved module to cover the #1 dropout point. (8) Explicitly warn about Next.js App Router churn and recommend pinning to 14+. (9) Add an a11y and security checklist to the production phase. Do not recommend specific state management libraries — keep that conditional. Avoid link dumps; focus on the project ladder.
